---
title: "3.3 Data Encapsulation and Instance Variables"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Data Encapsulation

### Implementation details are hidden

**Data encapsulation** (数据封装) is an object-oriented design technique in which the implementation details of a class are kept hidden from external classes.

The keywords `public` and `private` are called **access modifiers** (访问修饰符).

They affect how you can access classes, data, constructors, and methods.

## Public and Private

### Access modifiers

The keyword `public` means anyone can access this.

It allows access from classes outside the declaring class.

The keyword `private` restricts access to the declaring class.

Variables or methods marked `private` are only accessible inside the class where they are defined.

## Private Instance Variables

### Standard class design

In general, instance variables in a class should be declared `private` to ensure data encapsulation.

```java
private String name;
private String email;
private String phoneNumber;
```

## Instance Variables

### Object data

**Instance Variables** (sometimes called **attributes**, **fields**, or **properties**) hold the data for an object.

They belong to the object, and each object has its own copy of these variables.

On the AP CSA exam, instance variables should be declared **private**.

## Instance Variable Pattern

### Where declarations appear

Instance variables are declared right after the class declaration.

They usually start with `private`, then the **type** of the variable, and then a **name** for the variable.

```java
public class Person
{
    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phoneNumber;
}
```

## Person Variables

### Three pieces of data

The `Person` class declares three private instance variables:

::: {.tight-list}
- `name`
- `email`
- `phoneNumber`
:::

These are things that you might want to know about a person.

They are declared at the top of the class and exist inside the `{}` of the class.

## Class and Objects {.image-fit}

### Blueprint and separate object data

![Person class and objects](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-3-anatomy-of-a-class/PersonClassWithCookies.png){fig-align="center" width="78%"}

Each object will have its own copies of the same instance variables but with possibly different values in them.

## Encapsulation Practice

### Internal attributes, public methods

Access to attributes should be kept internal to the class in order to accomplish encapsulation.

It is good programming practice to designate the instance variables as `private`.

The data and the code acting on the data are wrapped together into a single unit, and the implementation details are hidden.

## Outside Classes

### Interact through public methods

Only code in the class can access or change the values of `private` instance variables.

Code in other classes can only interact with the `public` methods you provide.

When designing a class, you decide what data to make accessible or modifiable from other classes by what `public` methods you provide.

## Check Your Understanding

### `clickablearea:: name_instance_variables`

Click on all the instance variable declarations in the following class.

```java
public class Name
{
    private String first;
    private String last;

    public Name(String theFirst, String theLast)
    {
        first = theFirst;
        last = theLast;
    }

    public void setFirst(String theFirst)
    {
        first = theFirst;
    }

    public void setLast(String theLast)
    {
        last = theLast;
    }
}
```

## Answer Reasoning

### Instance variables are declarations after the class declaration

Correct lines:

```java
private String first;
private String last;
```

They are private declarations inside the class and outside any constructor or method.

The assignment statements `first = theFirst;` and `last = theLast;` are not declarations.

## Active Code

### `activecode:: PersonClass2`

The following `Person` class keeps track of the name, email, and phone number of a person.

What other data could you keep track of about a `Person`?

Add another private instance variable of your choice.

Do not worry about changing the constructor or other methods yet.

## PersonClass2 Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables
    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phoneNumber;
    // Add another private instance variable about a Person here


    // constructor: construct a Person copying in the data into the instance
    // variables
    public Person(String initName, String initEmail, String initPhone)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
        phoneNumber = initPhone;
    }

    // Print all the data for a person
    public void print()
    {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Email: " + email);
        System.out.println("Phone Number: " + phoneNumber);
    }

    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // call the constructor to create a new person
        Person p1 = new Person("Sana", "sana@gmail.com", "123-456-7890");
        // call p1's print method
        p1.print();
        Person p2 = new Person("Jean", "jean@gmail.com", "404 899-9955");
        p2.print();
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Target

### Runestone private-variable check

The Runestone test checks for four private instance variables after your addition.

```text
Expected: 4 Private
Check: Checking Private Instance Variable(s)
```

The task does not ask you to change the constructor or the `print` method yet.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define data encapsulation as hiding implementation details from external classes
- distinguish `public` access from `private` access
- state that instance variables should generally be `private`
- explain that each object has its own copy of instance variables
- identify instance variable declarations outside constructors and methods
- preserve starter blanks when adding one new private instance variable
:::

## End

### 3.3 Part 2 complete

Next: instance methods.
